content.text: What It Is

Load testing LLM API paths means driving an agentic application with synthetic concurrent traffic in order to measure how the system behaves as the number of simultaneous users, requests, or active conversations grows. Unlike load testing a traditional REST API — where the core question is requests per second — LLM API load testing must account for an additional resource dimension: tokens. Every call to an LLM API consumes input and output tokens, and providers enforce both a requests-per-minute (RPM) limit and a tokens-per-minute (TPM) limit independently. A single user sending a 4 000-token prompt followed by a 2 000-token completion can exhaust a TPM budget far faster than ten users exchanging brief messages.
The tools most commonly used for this are
Locust
(a Python-based, scriptable load generator) and
k6

(a JavaScript/Go tool with first-class custom metrics support). Both simulate virtual users that follow a realistic scenario — authenticating, selecting a prompt, sending a chat completion request, receiving a streaming or batch response, and then measuring how long each stage took. The test runner scales from a handful of users on a laptop to hundreds of thousands of virtual users distributed across a cloud fleet.

In an agentic application, an LLM API path is not a single HTTP call. It is a multi-turn loop: the model responds, tools are invoked, tool results are appended, and the model responds again. Each iteration of that loop adds latency and consumes tokens, which means the tail latency and throughput ceiling of the agentic system as a whole depends on how well the load test exercises the entire loop — not just a single completion endpoint.
Why It Matters

Production incidents at companies deploying LLM-powered features almost always have one of two root causes: rate limits were hit unexpectedly, or latency grew non-linearly under concurrency. Neither failure mode shows up in functional tests or unit tests, because those tests run sequentially with a single thread. The first time many teams discover that their agentic customer support system saturates its provider's TPM allocation at 50 simultaneous conversations — instead of the 500 they planned for — is during a launch event.

Load testing LLM API paths surfaces these problems before traffic is real. It answers questions that no other test category can: At what concurrency level do 429 rate-limit errors start appearing? How deep does the internal request queue grow before responses begin timing out? Does the first token in a streaming response arrive within 800 ms at 100 concurrent users but degrade to 3.5 seconds at 300? Is a multi-tool agent turn two to three times slower than a zero-tool turn, and does that gap widen under load?

The answers directly inform architectural decisions: whether to add a caching layer in front of the LLM, whether to implement a queue-and-worker pattern instead of synchronous passthrough, which tier of API access to purchase from the provider, and whether streaming is worth the added complexity of server-sent events in the client.
Benefits
Find the saturation point before production.
Load tests reveal the concurrency level at which 429 errors start and where p99 latency crosses SLA thresholds, giving teams a concrete number to design infrastructure around.
Validate retry and backoff logic under real pressure.
Exponential backoff code that looks correct in unit tests can have bugs that only manifest when multiple threads simultaneously hit a rate limit. A load test triggers those race conditions.
Measure realistic token throughput.
Tokens-per-second (TPS) is the correct throughput metric for LLM workloads. Load tests with representative prompt distributions yield a TPS figure that maps directly to provider tier selection.
Expose queue depth problems.
An agentic system that queues LLM requests internally will eventually stall if the queue grows faster than it drains. Load testing reveals the queue depth at which the system enters a degraded state.
Benchmark streaming vs. non-streaming tradeoffs.
Streaming provides faster time-to-first-token (TTFT) but adds server-side complexity. Load tests with both modes produce objective latency distributions to guide the choice.
Inform capacity planning and cost models.
The test run logs total tokens consumed, which feeds directly into monthly cost projections at projected user scale.
Trade-offs and Limitations

Load testing LLM APIs carries real monetary cost. Every virtual user call during a load test consumes tokens and is billed at standard rates. A 30-minute test with 100 concurrent users sending 500-token prompts and receiving 800-token responses can cost tens to hundreds of dollars depending on the model. Teams must budget for load testing explicitly and run large tests only when the system is close to production-ready.

Rate limits themselves complicate load testing. If the goal of a load test is to measure system behavior at high concurrency, but the provider rate-limits requests before the system under test is actually stressed, the load test is measuring the provider's throttling behavior rather than the application's. Teams must negotiate higher rate-limit tiers with providers before running meaningful capacity tests, or use a staging environment with a mock LLM gateway that simulates rate-limit responses deterministically.
Non-deterministic response lengths.

LLM responses vary in length, which makes token consumption per request stochastic. Load test results have more variance than traditional API tests, requiring longer runs or more repeated experiments to produce stable percentile estimates.
Provider infrastructure variability.

LLM provider latency varies based on their own infrastructure load. A load test run during provider peak hours will show higher latencies than a run at off-peak times, making comparison across runs less reliable.
Streaming adds measurement complexity.

Measuring TTFT and streaming chunk latency requires instrumenting the HTTP response body byte-by-byte, which most off-the-shelf load testing tools do not support natively. Custom instrumentation is required.
Prompt sensitivity.
The token budget and latency of a completion depend on the prompt. A load test using unrealistically short or simple prompts will underestimate real-world token consumption and latency.
How It Works
The fundamental unit of measurement in LLM load testing is the
virtual user (VU)

— a simulated client that repeats a scenario loop: pick a prompt, make an HTTP request to the LLM API path, receive the response (streaming or batch), record timing data, and optionally sleep before the next iteration. The load generator spawns hundreds or thousands of VUs simultaneously, each running independently. The aggregate of their requests constitutes the synthetic load.
Provider rate limits operate via a
token bucket model

. The provider maintains a bucket with a fixed capacity (for example, 90 000 tokens per minute). Each token consumed drains the bucket by that amount. The bucket refills at a steady rate — 90 000 tokens per 60 seconds, or 1 500 tokens per second. When the bucket is empty, subsequent requests receive a 429 Too Many Requests response, usually with a
Retry-After
or
x-ratelimit-reset-tokens

header indicating when the bucket will have enough capacity for the next request. There are separate buckets for RPM (requests) and TPM (tokens), and a request must fit within both budgets simultaneously.
A well-designed load test measures several distinct phases. During the
ramp-up phase
, VUs are added gradually (for example, 10 new VUs per 30 seconds) to identify the concurrency threshold at which latency begins to increase. During the
sustained load phase
, VU count is held constant at the target level to measure steady-state throughput, latency percentiles, and error rates. A
spike phase
adds a sudden burst of users to test how the system recovers when the token bucket drains abruptly and rate-limit responses flood in. A
soak phase
runs sustained load for hours to detect memory leaks in the queue manager or gradual degradation in retry logic.

For streaming endpoints, time-to-first-token (TTFT) is the most user-visible metric. It is measured as the elapsed time from sending the HTTP request to receiving the first server-sent-event (SSE) data chunk with a model token. Subsequent chunk latency — the gap between consecutive SSE events — determines how smooth the text appears to stream. Both are measured inside the VU's response handler by recording timestamps at each SSE boundary.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Load testing architecture for LLM API paths

content.text: The load generator runs virtual users that send HTTP requests to the LLM Gateway — the application layer that enforces rate limits, manages queues, routes to the correct model endpoint, and handles retries. The gateway forwards to two downstream targets: the LLM API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, or a self-hosted model) for completions, and external Tool APIs for search, database, or internal service calls. Metrics from all three layers (response times observed by the load generator, rate-limit headers from the LLM API, and queue depth from the gateway) flow to a metrics collector and then to an observability dashboard. The dashboard surfaces the four panels that matter most: throughput (tokens/sec and requests/min), latency (TTFT, p50, p95, p99), error rates (429s, timeouts, 5xx), and queue depth (pending and in-flight).
When to Use It

Run LLM API load tests before launching a feature to production users. The minimum useful test checks whether the system handles the expected peak concurrent-conversation count without degrading to unacceptable latency. A reasonable minimum SLA for an interactive agentic feature is TTFT under 1.5 seconds at p95 with zero 429 errors at target concurrency.
Run when scaling up user counts.
Any time the expected peak concurrent users doubles, re-run the load test. Non-linear behaviors (queue saturation, connection pool exhaustion) may not manifest until specific thresholds are crossed.
Run after changing prompt templates.

Longer prompts consume more input tokens per request. A prompt template change that adds 500 tokens of context increases TPM consumption proportionally and can cause a system that previously ran comfortably under rate limits to begin hitting them.
Run when switching models.

Different models have different latency profiles. GPT-4o is faster than GPT-4 Turbo for similar tasks; Claude claude-sonnet-4-6 has different token latency than Claude claude-haiku-4-5. Switching models without re-testing can invalidate SLA assumptions.
Do not run against production provider tiers without a spending limit set.
Uncontrolled load tests can consume significant token budgets in minutes. Always configure provider spending limits or use a dedicated load-testing API key with a tight quota.
Do not use load tests to validate functional correctness.
Load tests measure performance characteristics, not answer quality. Functional and evaluation testing require separate test runs with graded outputs.
How to Implement

A practical implementation follows five steps. First, define the load profile: the target concurrent-user count, the ramp duration, the sustained duration, and whether a spike or soak phase is needed. Second, build a representative prompt corpus — a collection of prompts that reflects the real distribution the system will receive in production, including the range of input lengths, the fraction of requests that trigger tool calls, and the fraction of multi-turn vs. single-turn conversations. A corpus of 50–200 prompts is usually sufficient for stable averages.
Third, instrument the VU scenario to capture the metrics that matter: wall-clock request duration, TTFT (for streaming), total tokens consumed (read from the response body or from
x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens

headers), and the HTTP status code. Fourth, define success thresholds — for example, p95 response time must be below 3 seconds and the 429 error rate must be below 0.5% — and configure the test runner to fail the run if thresholds are breached. Fifth, collect provider-side metrics by polling the
x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens
and
x-ratelimit-remaining-requests
response headers on each call and emitting them as custom metrics to the dashboard.
Install the load generator:
pip install locust
or download the k6 binary.
Build a prompt corpus file (CSV or JSON) with at least 30 representative prompts covering short, medium, and long token lengths.
Write the VU scenario script, handling 429 responses with exponential backoff inside the scenario — not relying on the load generator's default error counting.
Run a short smoke test (5 VUs, 2 minutes) to validate the script before committing to a full run.
Run the full load profile and export results to a persistent store (InfluxDB, CSV, k6 Cloud, or Locust's built-in CSV export).
Compare results against the defined SLA thresholds and file tickets for any regressions.
Examples
Locust: Basic LLM API Load Test

The following Locust locustfile simulates users sending chat completion requests to an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, measures response times, handles 429 rate-limit errors with exponential backoff, and tracks total tokens consumed as a custom counter.

# locustfile.py — LLM API load test with Locust
# pip install locust openai

import random
import time
import json
from locust import HttpUser, task, between, events
from locust.runners import MasterRunner
import gevent

# Representative prompt corpus — use real prompts from your system in production
PROMPT_CORPUS = [
    "Summarize the key differences between supervised and unsupervised learning in two sentences.",
    "Write a Python function that computes the nth Fibonacci number iteratively.",
    "What are the main causes of the 2008 financial crisis? Provide a brief overview.",
    "Explain the concept of attention in transformer models at a high level.",
    "Draft a short professional email declining a meeting due to a scheduling conflict.",
    "List three advantages of containerization in software deployment.",
    "What is the difference between TCP and UDP? When would you use each?",
    "Write SQL to find the top 5 customers by total order value from an orders table.",
    "Explain how gradient descent works in one paragraph.",
    "Describe the CAP theorem and what it means for distributed systems.",
]

# Track total tokens consumed across all users
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Run with:
locust -f locustfile.py --users 50 --spawn-rate 5 --run-time 5m --headless --csv results
. The
--headless
flag runs without the web UI; omit it to use the interactive dashboard during development.
k6: LLM API Load Test with Custom Metrics
k6 uses JavaScript for scenario scripts and has first-class support for custom metrics. The following script tracks TTFT for streaming responses and emits tokens-per-second as a rate metric.
Measuring TTFT for Streaming Endpoints
Streaming responses require byte-level instrumentation. The following Python example measures TTFT by detecting the first non-empty SSE data chunk in a streaming completion response.

# streaming_ttft.py — measure time-to-first-token for streaming completions
# pip install openai httpx
import time
import httpx
import json

API_KEY = "sk-YOUR-KEY"
BASE_URL = "https://api.openai.com"

def measure_streaming_ttft(prompt: str, model: str = "gpt-4o-mini") -> dict:
    """
    Returns a dict with keys:
      ttft_ms     - time from request send to first token received
      total_ms    - total time for full response
      chunks      - number of SSE events received
      total_tokens - approximate total tokens (from final usage chunk if present)
    """
    payload = {
        "model": model,
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        "max_tokens": 512,
        "stream": True,
        "stream_options": {"include_usage": True},
    }
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
    }

    ttft_ms = None
    chunk_count = 0
    total_tokens = 0
    start = time.monotonic()

    with httpx.stream(
        "POST",
        f"{BASE_URL}/v1/chat/completions",
        json=payload,
        headers=headers,
        timeout=60.0,
    ) as response:
        response.raise_for_status()
        for line in response.iter_lines():
            line = line.strip()
            if not line or line == "data: [DONE]":
                continue
            if line.startswith("data: "):
                data_str = line[len("data: "):]
                try:
                    chunk = json.loads(data_str)
                except json.JSONDecodeError:
                    continue

                # Check for the first content token
                choices = chunk.get("choices", [])
                if choices and ttft_ms is None:
                    delta = choices[0].get("delta", {})
                    if delta.get("content"):
                        ttft_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000

                chunk_count += 1

                # The final usage chunk (when stream_options.include_usage=True)
                if "usage" in chunk and chunk["usage"]:
                    total_tokens = chunk["usage"].get("total_tokens", 0)

    total_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
    return {
        "ttft_ms": round(ttft_ms or 0, 1),
        "total_ms": round(total_ms, 1),
        "chunks": chunk_count,
        "total_tokens": total_tokens,
    }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = measure_streaming_ttft("Explain the concept of entropy in three sentences.")
    print(f"TTFT: {result['ttft_ms']} ms")
    print(f"Total time: {result['total_ms']} ms")
    print(f"Chunks received: {result['chunks']}")
    print(f"Total tokens: {result['total_tokens']}")
Queue Depth Testing

Agentic systems often queue LLM requests to prevent overwhelming the provider. The following example tests queue behavior by submitting bursts of requests and monitoring queue depth through an internal health endpoint.

# queue_depth_test.py — test internal queue behavior under burst load
# Assumes your agentic service exposes GET /health/queue returning
# {"pending": N, "in_flight": N, "rejected": N}
import asyncio
import aiohttp
import time
from statistics import mean, median, quantiles

SERVICE_URL = "http://localhost:8080"
CONCURRENCY = 80   # simultaneous requests
TOTAL_REQUESTS = 400

async def send_request(session: aiohttp.ClientSession, prompt: str) -> dict:
    start = time.monotonic()
    try:
        async with session.post(
            f"{SERVICE_URL}/api/chat",
            json={"message": prompt},
            timeout=aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=60),
        ) as resp:
            status = resp.status
            await resp.read()
            return {"status": status, "latency_ms": (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000}
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        return {"status": 504, "latency_ms": 60_000}
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Exponential Backoff with Jitter for Rate-Limit Handling

The following utility implements the full-jitter exponential backoff pattern recommended for LLM API rate limits. It reads the provider-supplied retry delay from response headers when available and falls back to computed backoff otherwise.

# backoff.py — production-grade retry with full-jitter backoff for LLM APIs
import time
import random
import httpx
from typing import Callable, Any

def call_with_backoff(
    fn: Callable[[], httpx.Response],
    max_retries: int = 6,
    base_delay: float = 1.0,
    max_delay: float = 60.0,
) -> httpx.Response:
    """
    Call fn() and retry on HTTP 429 or 503 with full-jitter exponential backoff.

    Full jitter: wait = random.uniform(0, min(cap, base * 2**attempt))
    This avoids the thundering-herd problem where many clients retry
    simultaneously after a rate limit, re-saturating the provider immediately.

    The provider may include a Retry-After header (seconds) or
    x-ratelimit-reset-tokens header (epoch timestamp). If present, wait at
    least that long before retrying.
    """
    for attempt in range(max_retries + 1):
        response = fn()

        if response.status_code not in (429, 503):
            return response  # success or non-retriable error

        if attempt == max_retries:
            return response  # exhausted retries, return the error response

        # Determine how long to wait
        provider_wait = 0.0

        retry_after = response.headers.get("retry-after")
        if retry_after:
            try:
                provider_wait = float(retry_after)
            except ValueError:
                pass

        # Full-jitter backoff
        cap = min(max_delay, base_delay * (2 ** attempt))
        jitter_wait = random.uniform(0, cap)

        wait = max(provider_wait, jitter_wait)
        print(f"Rate limited (attempt {attempt + 1}/{max_retries}). Waiting {wait:.2f}s")
        time.sleep(wait)

    return response  # unreachable, satisfies type checker


# Usage example:
# with httpx.Client() as client:
#     response = call_with_backoff(
#         lambda: client.post(
#             "https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions",
#             json=payload,
#             headers=headers,
#         )
#     )
Real-World Example

Consider an agentic customer support system deployed for an e-commerce platform. The system handles inbound support queries: each conversation is an agentic loop that may invoke a product database lookup tool, an order-status API tool, and a returns-policy retrieval tool before generating a final response. The product team expects up to 300 simultaneous conversations during peak shopping hours.

Before launch, the team runs a Locust load test with a scenario that mirrors real traffic: 40% of conversations are simple one-turn queries (average 200 input tokens, 150 output tokens), 45% require one tool call (average 400 input tokens across two LLM calls, 200 output tokens total), and 15% are complex multi-tool conversations (average 800 input tokens across three calls, 350 output tokens). They build a corpus of 80 representative support queries and weight the selection probabilities to match those fractions.

Running the test at 100 concurrent conversations (one third of peak), the team observes clean behavior: p95 response time is 2.1 seconds and 429 error rate is 0.0%. At 200 concurrent conversations, p95 rises to 3.8 seconds — still within the 5-second SLA — but they see occasional 429 bursts lasting 10–15 seconds caused by tool-call-heavy conversations exhausting the token budget during a short window.

At 300 concurrent conversations, the 429 rate reaches 4% and the queue in the gateway grows to 80 pending requests, causing p99 latency to spike above 12 seconds. The root cause is that the token bucket algorithm does not account for the bursty nature of multi-tool conversations: a single 800-token input burst drains 0.89% of the per-minute TPM budget in a single second.

The fix is a token-aware request scheduler in the gateway: before submitting an LLM call, the scheduler estimates the request's token count from the message history and checks the current remaining-tokens budget (available via the
x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens

response header from the previous call). If the bucket is below a safety threshold, the request waits 200 ms and retries the budget check. This throttles the submission rate internally without returning errors to users. After deploying the scheduler and re-running the load test, p99 at 300 concurrent conversations drops to 5.2 seconds with a 0.1% 429 rate — within acceptable bounds.
Common Mistakes
Using Unrealistic Prompt Lengths

Load tests that use a single short prompt ("Hello!") for every virtual user vastly underestimate token consumption and latency. A 10-token input prompt takes a fraction of the processing time of a 2 000-token context window. The rate-limit headroom visible in a short-prompt test disappears when real users send long conversation histories. Always build a prompt corpus that reflects the actual input length distribution, including tail cases with large context windows.
Ignoring the Token Dimension of Rate Limits

Teams that focus only on requests-per-minute are often surprised when they hit TPM limits well before the RPM limit. A system with a 60 000 TPM limit and a 500 RPM limit can hit the TPM ceiling with as few as 120 requests per minute if each request uses 500 tokens on average. Always track both RPM and TPM consumption during load tests and report both utilization percentages.
Treating 429 Responses as Test Failures Without Context

A 429 response during a load test is not necessarily a bug in the application — it may be the correct behavior when the test intentionally exceeds the provider's rate limit. The correct approach is to separate 429s that happen inside the retry window (expected and handled by backoff) from 429s that escape to the user (a failure). Instrument the backoff code to distinguish these two categories and report them separately in the metrics dashboard.
Running Load Tests Against Production

Running an uncontrolled load test against a production API key and production infrastructure can exhaust a monthly token budget in minutes, degrade service for real users, and generate a large unexpected bill. Always use a dedicated load-testing API key with a spending limit, and run tests against a staging environment that mirrors production architecture but is isolated from live traffic.
Not Testing Tool-Call Paths Separately

In an agentic system, a tool-calling turn is two LLM API calls — the first to generate the tool call, and the second to generate the final response after the tool result is appended. The latency of a tool turn is therefore roughly double the latency of a zero-tool turn, plus the latency of the tool API call itself. Testing only zero-tool scenarios will produce latency numbers that are optimistic by a factor of two or more for the majority of real conversations.
Best Practices
Build a representative prompt corpus.
Sample 50–200 real prompts from production logs (anonymized). Weight their selection probabilities in the VU scenario to match the actual distribution of short, medium, and long inputs.
Track tokens per second as the primary throughput metric.
Requests-per-second is a secondary metric for LLM workloads. Total tokens consumed divided by test duration gives TPS, which maps directly to provider TPM limits and cost projections.
Use staged load profiles, not step functions.
Ramp VUs gradually (e.g., 10 new VUs every 30 seconds) to identify the exact concurrency threshold at which behavior changes, rather than jumping directly to peak load.
Test streaming and non-streaming separately.
They exercise different code paths and have different TTFT characteristics. Maintain separate test scenarios for each mode and report their metrics independently.
Read rate-limit headers on every response.
The
x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens
x-ratelimit-remaining-requests
, and
x-ratelimit-reset-tokens
headers are available on every OpenAI and Anthropic response. Emit them as custom metrics to visualize token bucket utilization in real time during the test.
Implement full-jitter backoff, not fixed backoff.
Fixed backoff causes all retrying VUs to wake up simultaneously and re-saturate the rate limit. Full-jitter spreads retries randomly across the backoff window, reducing the thundering-herd effect.
Define and enforce SLA thresholds in the test runner.
Use k6 thresholds or Locust's
environment.stats.total.fail_ratio
to automatically fail the test run if p95 latency or error rate exceeds defined limits, making load tests part of CI gates.
Save results to a time-series store.
Export Locust CSV or k6 JSON results to InfluxDB or a similar store so that load test results can be compared across releases and trends identified over time.
Performance and Scalability

Throughput for an agentic LLM system scales along two independent axes: the application layer's concurrency capacity (connections, goroutines, async tasks) and the provider's rate-limit budget. Because these two ceilings are independent, the system can hit either one first depending on its architecture. A well-provisioned agentic gateway can handle hundreds of concurrent in-flight connections to the LLM API, but will be throttled well before that by a standard-tier TPM limit.

Token consumption accumulates non-linearly in multi-turn agents. A five-turn conversation does not consume five times the tokens of a one-turn conversation, because each subsequent turn appends the full prior context. Turn 1 might use 200 input tokens; turn 2 uses 200 (prior) + 150 (prior output) + 200 (new input) = 550 input tokens; turn 3 uses 550 + 400 + 200 = 1 150. The total grows quadratically with the number of turns, making long-running agents disproportionately expensive under load.
Horizontal scaling of the gateway.

The application gateway scales horizontally, but the provider rate limit is per API key. Multiple gateway instances sharing one key do not multiply the token budget. To scale beyond a single key's limits, use multiple API keys with load balancing across them, or use multiple provider accounts.
Context truncation as a throughput lever.

Truncating old turns in the conversation history before appending a new message caps input token growth per turn, trading response quality for throughput headroom. Load tests should run with and without truncation enabled to quantify the throughput improvement.
Caching for repeated prompts.

If a significant fraction of incoming prompts are semantically similar (e.g., a support system where many users ask the same FAQ), a semantic caching layer (such as GPTCache or a custom embedding-similarity cache) can serve cached responses without consuming provider tokens. Load tests on the cached vs. uncached path quantify the cache's token savings.
Provider tier selection.

Most LLM providers offer multiple tiers with different RPM and TPM limits. Load test results translate directly to tier requirements: if the test shows steady-state consumption of 45 000 TPM at 200 concurrent users, the team needs a tier with at least 60 000 TPM (adding a 33% headroom buffer).

Logic correctness, not performance

A mock-gateway approach is invaluable for testing application-layer queuing and retry logic without paying provider costs. The gateway can be configured to return 429 responses at a controlled rate and with configurable latency, allowing the application's backoff logic to be tested under realistic conditions at zero token cost. Once the application layer is validated against the mock gateway, a smaller live-API test confirms that end-to-end latency at the real provider matches expectations.
Security and Reliability
Load test scripts almost always contain API keys. These must never be committed to source control. Use environment variables (
OPENAI_API_KEY
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

) or a secrets manager. The API key used for load testing should be a dedicated key distinct from the production key, with its own spending limit set at the provider dashboard. If the load test script is accidentally run against production infrastructure, the spending limit prevents runaway costs.

Rate-limit responses (429) during a load test are information, not errors to suppress. Log every 429 with its timestamp, the VU identifier, the remaining-tokens header value, and the retry delay used. This trace is essential for diagnosing whether the rate-limit strategy (backoff duration, bucket replenishment timing) is working correctly. A 429 that triggers correct backoff and eventually succeeds is a healthy signal; a 429 that causes the VU to immediately retry and generate a cascade of 429s is a bug in the retry logic.
Set provider spending limits before running any load test.
A misconfigured concurrency setting can generate thousands of dollars of token charges in minutes. Most providers allow per-key monthly spending limits.
Isolate load test environments.
Use a staging deployment that is network-isolated from production. The load generator's synthetic traffic must not share a database, cache, or queue with real user traffic.
Test circuit breakers explicitly.

If the system uses a circuit breaker to shed LLM load when the error rate exceeds a threshold, the load test must exercise the condition that trips the circuit (sustained 429 rate) and verify that the system gracefully degrades (returns cached responses or queues requests) rather than throwing unhandled exceptions.
Verify graceful shutdown under load.

Stop the load test abruptly (simulate a process kill) and verify that the gateway drains its queue cleanly, in-flight requests complete or time out gracefully, and no orphaned requests are left open against the provider.
Audit logs for sensitive data in prompts.
Load test prompts should be synthetic, not sampled from real user conversations, to avoid logging personally identifiable information or confidential business content in test infrastructure logs.
Key Takeaways
LLM API load testing must measure two resource dimensions — requests per minute (RPM) and tokens per minute (TPM) — because either can be the binding constraint at different concurrency levels.

Locust and k6 are the primary tools; Locust excels for Python-native scenarios with direct SDK integration, while k6 is better suited for CI-gated threshold enforcement and cloud-distributed execution.

Streaming endpoints require custom byte-level instrumentation to measure time-to-first-token (TTFT); standard load testing request-duration metrics capture total response time, not the user-visible first-token delay.

The token bucket model governs provider rate limiting: requests are throttled when the bucket drains, not when request count alone hits a ceiling, making prompt length distribution the most important variable in the load test design.
Tool-calling turns consume roughly twice the tokens and take twice as long as zero-tool turns; load test scenarios must include the tool-call path weighted by its real-world occurrence rate.

Full-jitter exponential backoff prevents the thundering-herd problem when multiple VUs are rate-limited simultaneously; fixed or linear backoff causes synchronized retry storms that re-saturate the token bucket immediately.
Use a dedicated API key with a provider spending limit for load tests; run against a staging environment isolated from production traffic; never commit API keys to the test scripts.

Load test results map directly to architecture decisions: token-aware request schedulers, semantic caches, context truncation policies, and provider tier selection all derive their justification from measured saturation points and throughput curves.
